items.q: Is a tennis serve a closed or open skill?
items.correct: Closed - performed in a predictable, self-paced environment.
items.misconception: Open - because tennis is a fast-paced sport.
items.why: Students judge by the sport, not the moment. The serve happens at the server's pace with a stationary ball - closed. The return of serve is open. Skill classification depends on the SPECIFIC action's environment.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.misconception: Skills are unique and not generalisable
items.why: The answer examiners want is: GMPs modified by parameters. “Skills are unique and not generalisable” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Recall and recognition schemas. “Reaction time only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If 100 practice attempts are split 80/20 between two conditions, additional condition trials are?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
